does: This paper introduces Game Arena, an open-source evaluation platform designed to benchmark AI systems through direct head-to-head competition in game environments with well-defined objectives and winning conditions. The platform enables rigorous comparative measurement of frontier AI models by placing them in adversarial or competitive settings where performance outcomes are unambiguous, providing quantitative metrics beyond traditional benchmarks. The core contribution is establishing an infrastructure that standardizes AI evaluation through game-based competition, allowing researchers to assess capabilities across diverse strategic domains with transparent scoring mechanisms.
connects: This work builds on a long tradition of using games as evaluation frameworks for artificial intelligence, extending from chess engines and AlphaGo to broader competitive testing paradigms. It responds to growing concerns about limitations of static benchmark suites that may saturate, suffer from data contamination, or fail to capture dynamic problem-solving under adversarial conditions. The research draws on comparative evaluation methodologies from the multi-agent reinforcement learning literature and addresses calls for more robust, contestable benchmarks that go beyond fixed test sets. It combines insights from competitive game theory with practical concerns about fairly assessing increasingly capable frontier models.
enables: By providing standardized game-based evaluation infrastructure, this work enables future research into comparative AI capabilities and facilitates the development of more transparent leaderboards that reflect head-to-head performance. The platform could support discovery of which capabilities matter for real-world performance and which emerge under competitive pressure. It establishes scaffolding for continuous evaluation as models improve, avoiding benchmark saturation and enabling the community to identify capability gaps through adversarial testing. The open-source nature allows downstream work on custom game domains, integration with existing model evaluation pipelines, and potential applications to safety testing through games designed to probe alignment and robustness.
